description: Screen shows a problem description panel. Title reads 'Task 1: Two Sum'. Body text visible: 'Given an array of integers, return the two numbers that add up to a specific target.' Examples section below with input/output pairs. Scrollbar near top.

Same problem description panel. User has scrolled down. Constraints section now visible: 'Each input has exactly one solution' and 'You may not use the same element twice'. A code editor tab is visible but not active in the tab bar.
Still on problem description. No visible changes from previous frame. Cursor not visible.

Screen changed to code editor. File tab shows 'solution.py'. Editor contains a pre-filled stub: 'def two_sum(nums, target):' followed by '    pass' on the next line. Cursor is blinking at end of line 2. Problem description now collapsed into a side panel.
Code editor. The 'pass' text has been deleted. User has typed 'for i in range(len(nums)):' on line 2. Cursor at end of line 2.
Code editor. New line 3 added: '        for j in range(len(nums)):'. Cursor at end of line 3. Indentation appears to be 8 spaces.
Code editor. Line 4 added: '            if nums[i] + nums[j] == target:'. Cursor at end of line 4.
Code editor. Line 5 added: '                return [i, j]'. Full code visible in editor is 5 lines. No other changes.
Code editor. No code changes. Mouse cursor has moved toward the bottom right area of the screen where a green 'Run' button is visible.
Code editor with test output panel now visible below. Panel shows: 'Test 1: PASS ✓' in green, 'Test 2: FAIL ✗ — expected [0, 2], got [0, 0]' in red, 'Test 3: PASS ✓' in green.
Code editor. Same test output still visible below. No typing. Mouse cursor is hovering over line 3 of the code ('for j in range(len(nums))'). No edits made across several frames.
Code editor. Line 3 has been modified. Previously read 'for j in range(len(nums)):', now reads 'for j in range(i + 1, len(nums)):'. Cursor at end of line 3.
Code editor with test output panel. Output now shows: 'Test 1: PASS ✓', 'Test 2: PASS ✓', 'Test 3: PASS ✓'. All green.
Code editor. Same passing test results visible. No code changes. No mouse movement detected. Cursor blinking on line 5.

Screen has changed. A chat panel is now visible on the right side of the screen, labeled 'AI Assistant'. The code editor is still visible on the left but narrower. User has typed in the chat input box: 'can this be done in O(n) time?'

Chat panel shows a response from the assistant. Message bubble reads: 'Yes — you can use a hash map. As you iterate through the array, store each number's index in a dictionary. For each element, check if (target - current) already exists in the dictionary. This gives you O(n) time and O(n) space.' User's original message visible above it.
Code editor is now full width again, chat panel closed. User has selected all code in the editor (lines 2-5 highlighted in blue).
Code editor. Previous code deleted. Line 2 now reads: 'seen = {}'. Cursor at end of line 2.
Code editor. Lines 2-4 now read: '    seen = {}' / '    for i, num in enumerate(nums):' / '        complement = target - num'. Cursor at end of line 4.

Code editor. Full code now visible: 'def two_sum(nums, target):' / '    seen = {}' / '    for i, num in enumerate(nums):' / '        complement = target - num' / '        if complement in seen:' / '            return [seen[complement], i]' / '        seen[num] = i'. Cursor at end of line 7.
Code editor with test output panel. All tests passing: 'Test 1: PASS ✓', 'Test 2: PASS ✓', 'Test 3: PASS ✓'. Green checkmarks.
A modal/overlay has appeared over the editor. Text reads 'Submit Solution?'. Two buttons visible: 'Cancel' and 'Submit'. Mouse cursor near the Submit button.
Modal now shows: 'All 12/12 test cases passed!' with a green checkmark icon. Below: 'Task 1 Complete'. A 'Continue to Task 2' button is visible.

Screen shows a new problem description panel. Title reads 'Task 2: Unique Characters'. Body text: 'Implement a function to determine if a string has all unique characters.' Examples section visible below.

Same problem description. User has scrolled down. A constraints section is visible reading: 'Character comparison should be case-insensitive.' Test examples show: is_unique('abcdef') → True, is_unique('aabcde') → False, is_unique('aAbBcC') → True.
Code editor now active. File tab shows 'solution2.py'. Pre-filled stub: 'def is_unique(s):' followed by '    pass'. Cursor at line 2.

Code editor. Code has been written across lines 2-6: '    seen = set()' / '    for char in s:' / '        if char in seen:' / '            return False' / '        seen.add(char)' / '    return True'. Cursor at end of line 7.
Code editor with test output panel. Results: 'Test 1: PASS ✓', 'Test 2: PASS ✓', 'Test 3: FAIL ✗ — is_unique("aAbBcC") expected True, got False'. First two green, last one red.

Screen changed to problem description panel. User appears to be re-reading the constraints. The text 'Character comparison should be case-insensitive' is visible. Below that the test case 'is_unique("aAbBcC") → True' is visible.
Chat panel open on right side. User has typed: 'wait so case insensitive means aAbB has duplicates right?'. Code editor visible on left.

Chat panel shows assistant response: 'Correct — case-insensitive comparison treats "a" and "A" as the same character. So "aAbB" would have duplicates and should return False.' User message visible above.

Problem description panel visible again. User appears to be scrolling between the constraint text and the test case. The constraint says 'case-insensitive' but test expects is_unique('aAbBcC') → True. No navigation away from this screen for several frames.
Chat panel open. New message from user: 'the problem says case insensitive but the test expects aAbBcC to be unique, that contradicts?'. Previous messages still visible above in the chat history.

Chat panel shows new assistant response: 'Good catch. The test case suggests case-sensitive comparison is expected despite the wording. I\'d go with what the test cases show — treat uppercase and lowercase as different characters. The constraint note may be misleading or poorly worded.' User scrolls slightly in the chat panel. Code editor visible on left with no changes.
